examples.title: Unification Algorithm
examples.description: Standard unification algorithm with occurs check
examples.code:
FUNCTION unify(term1, term2, θ):
 # θ is accumulated substitution
 IF θ is failure THEN return failure
 
 # Apply current substitution
 t1 := apply(θ, term1)
 t2 := apply(θ, term2)
 
 # Base case: terms identical
 IF t1 = t2 THEN return θ
 
 # If t1 is variable
 IF isVariable(t1) THEN
 IF occursCheck(t1, t2) THEN return extend(θ, t1, t2)
 ELSE return failure
 ENDIF
 
 # If t2 is variable
 IF isVariable(t2) THEN
 IF occursCheck(t2, t1) THEN return extend(θ, t2, t1)
 ELSE return failure
 ENDIF
 
 # If both are compounds
 IF isCompound(t1) AND isCompound(t2) THEN
 IF functor(t1) ≠ functor(t2) OR arity(t1) ≠ arity(t2) THEN
 return failure
 ENDIF
 # Unify arguments pairwise
 args1 := arguments(t1)
 args2 := arguments(t2)
 θ' := unifyLists(args1, args2, θ)
 RETURN θ'
 ENDIF
 
 # Otherwise failure
 RETURN failure

FUNCTION occursCheck(var, term):
 IF var ∈ variables(term) THEN return false
 ELSE return true
examples.explanation: The algorithm recursively unifies terms, applying substitutions along the way. It checks type compatibility, performs occurs check for variable bindings, and handles compound terms by unifying arguments pairwise.

examples.title: Prolog Unification Examples
examples.code:
% Fact
likes(john, mary).

% Rule
loves(X, Y) :- likes(Y, X).

% Unification examples (try in Prolog):
% ?- f(a, X) = f(a, b).
% Result: X = b

% ?- f(X, Y) = f(a, b).
% Result: X = a, Y = b

% ?- f(X, X) = f(a, b).
% Result: false (X cannot be both a and b)

% ?- X = f(X). % Prolog allows circular term
% Result: X = f(f(f(...))) % infinite structure

% ?- p(X, Y) = p(A, B), X = a, Y = b.
% Result: X = a, A = a, Y = b, B = b
examples.explanation: Shows various unification scenarios in Prolog: simple variable binding, multiple bindings, failure when conflicts arise, and Prolog's circular term creation (occurs check disabled).

examples.title: Composition of Substitutions
examples.code:
FUNCTION compose(θ, σ):
 # Apply σ first, then θ
 # Result: { X → θ(σ(X)) | X in domain of σ or affected by θ }
 
 result := empty substitution
 
 # First apply σ to all terms in θ
 FOR EACH (X, t) IN θ:
 new_term := apply(σ, t)
 result := add(result, X, new_term)
 ENDFOR
 
 # Add bindings from σ not affected by θ
 FOR EACH (Y, s) IN σ:
 IF Y not in domain(θ) THEN
 result := add(result, Y, s)
 ENDIF
 ENDFOR
 
 RETURN result

# Example:
# θ = {X → f(Y)}
# σ = {Y → a}
# compose(θ, σ) = {X → f(a), Y → a}
examples.explanation: Composition combines two substitutions, where the second substitution is applied to the terms of the first. The result is also normalized to be idempotent.
